critic_raw:
This fMRI-based Alzheimer's classification experiment has several critical methodological problems.

First, the preprocessing pipeline for the unsupervised branch states that 'functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' Feature standardization is also 'calculated on the full data set.' This is data leakage: the PCA components fitted on test participants' fMRI data influence the feature space used to train the classifier. Functional connectivity patterns from test participants—which encode their clinical labels—contaminate the unsupervised feature extraction.

Second, the design describes using a 'randomized stratified split' on a 5-year longitudinal cohort. The cohort has '5,000 scans representing 1,000–1,500 unique participants,' meaning most participants contribute multiple scans. A random split at the scan level will place different scans from the same participant in both train and test—participant-level leakage. The model will implicitly learn patient-specific fMRI fingerprints during training and be 'tested' on the same participants. Classification accuracy will be inflated relative to the real deployment scenario (classifying new, unseen participants). Splits must be participant-level, not scan-level.

Third, k-means clustering is applied to fMRI features, and cluster assignments become 'categorical features for downstream classification.' This is a two-stage supervised pipeline where the cluster labels are optimized using silhouette scores on the validation set. This is not unsupervised learning in any meaningful sense—the downstream classifier is supervised, and cluster k is selected to maximize validation performance. The 'unsupervised' label in the hypothesis is therefore misleading; the full pipeline is a semi-supervised feature extraction followed by supervised classification.

Fourth, the cognitive test baseline uses 'age-adjusted MMSE or MoCA total scores' as features. These scores are themselves derived from standardized cognitive assessments that are the clinical standard for Alzheimer's staging. The comparison is not 'fMRI vs. standard-of-care'—it is 'fMRI features (clustered) vs. the same clinical features used to assign the labels.' The labels are assigned partly based on neuropsychological batteries that overlap with the 'baseline' features, creating circular evaluation.

Fifth, the design proposes using 'paired-sample t-tests or McNemar test' on the test set. These assume paired observations (same patients evaluated by both models). However, the two models receive different feature sets (fMRI clusters vs. cognitive scores)—all patients are evaluated by both, so pairing is appropriate. But t-tests assume continuous outcomes; comparing classification accuracy between two models requires McNemar's test on paired binary outcomes, not t-tests. The statistical test specification is inconsistent.
all_issues_raised: Functional connectivity computation and PCA/ICA fit on entire dataset before splitting constitutes data leakage; test participant fMRI patterns contaminate feature extraction.

Random split at scan level on a longitudinal multi-scan cohort creates participant-level leakage; different scans from the same participant appear in both train and test, inflating performance estimates.
k-means clustering k is selected via validation set silhouette scores in a downstream supervised pipeline; calling this 'unsupervised' learning is misleading—it is semi-supervised feature extraction.

Cognitive test baseline features overlap with the neuropsychological batteries used to assign clinical labels, creating circularity in evaluation: the model is tested against features used to construct the outcome.
Statistical comparison specifies 'paired-sample t-tests or McNemar test' inconsistently; t-tests are inappropriate for comparing binary classification outcomes, which require McNemar's test.

round_transcripts.critic:
This fMRI-based Alzheimer's classification experiment has several critical methodological problems.

First, the preprocessing pipeline for the unsupervised branch states that 'functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' Feature standardization is also 'calculated on the full data set.' This is data leakage: the PCA components fitted on test participants' fMRI data influence the feature space used to train the classifier. Functional connectivity patterns from test participants—which encode their clinical labels—contaminate the unsupervised feature extraction.

Second, the design describes using a 'randomized stratified split' on a 5-year longitudinal cohort. The cohort has '5,000 scans representing 1,000–1,500 unique participants,' meaning most participants contribute multiple scans. A random split at the scan level will place different scans from the same participant in both train and test—participant-level leakage. The model will implicitly learn patient-specific fMRI fingerprints during training and be 'tested' on the same participants. Classification accuracy will be inflated relative to the real deployment scenario (classifying new, unseen participants). Splits must be participant-level, not scan-level.

Third, k-means clustering is applied to fMRI features, and cluster assignments become 'categorical features for downstream classification.' This is a two-stage supervised pipeline where the cluster labels are optimized using silhouette scores on the validation set. This is not unsupervised learning in any meaningful sense—the downstream classifier is supervised, and cluster k is selected to maximize validation performance. The 'unsupervised' label in the hypothesis is therefore misleading; the full pipeline is a semi-supervised feature extraction followed by supervised classification.

Fourth, the cognitive test baseline uses 'age-adjusted MMSE or MoCA total scores' as features. These scores are themselves derived from standardized cognitive assessments that are the clinical standard for Alzheimer's staging. The comparison is not 'fMRI vs. standard-of-care'—it is 'fMRI features (clustered) vs. the same clinical features used to assign the labels.' The labels are assigned partly based on neuropsychological batteries that overlap with the 'baseline' features, creating circular evaluation.

Fifth, the design proposes using 'paired-sample t-tests or McNemar test' on the test set. These assume paired observations (same patients evaluated by both models). However, the two models receive different feature sets (fMRI clusters vs. cognitive scores)—all patients are evaluated by both, so pairing is appropriate. But t-tests assume continuous outcomes; comparing classification accuracy between two models requires McNemar's test on paired binary outcomes, not t-tests. The statistical test specification is inconsistent.
round_transcripts.defender:
The critique raises genuinely serious concerns, particularly Issues 1 and 2.

On Issue 1 (PCA/functional connectivity leakage): The defender cannot dispute this. PCA fitted on the entire dataset including test participants means test-set brain patterns shape the feature space. This is leakage from the label-bearing test observations. All dimensionality reduction and connectivity computation should be fit on training participants only and applied to validation and test participants.

On Issue 2 (scan-level vs participant-level split): This is the most consequential flaw. In a longitudinal study where participants contribute multiple scans over 5 years, scan-level random splitting places the same participant in training and test. An fMRI classifier can learn participant-specific connectivity fingerprints that generalize across that participant's scans without generalizing to new patients. All splits must be at the participant level. A randomized split where all scans from a given participant are assigned to exactly one partition is required.

On Issue 3 (semi-supervised pipeline): The characterization of k-means clustering as 'unsupervised' is technically accurate for the clustering step, but the end-to-end pipeline is supervised. The hypothesis should be reframed as 'unsupervised feature extraction followed by supervised classification vs. cognitive tests + supervised classification.' This is a framing issue that does not invalidate the experiment but should be disclosed.

On Issue 4 (circular evaluation): This is a legitimate concern that requires careful examination. If Alzheimer's labels are assigned partly using MMSE scores, and MMSE scores are used as baseline features, the comparison asks whether fMRI outperforms a feature that helps define the outcome. This is a circularity that inflates baseline performance and potentially understates the fMRI model's comparative advantage.

On Issue 5 (t-test vs McNemar): The defender agrees—McNemar's test is the correct paired comparison for binary classification outcomes. T-tests should not be applied to accuracy comparisons between two binary classifiers.

round_transcripts.critic:
Remaining unresolved issues:

Issue 1 (preprocessing leakage): Defender concedes. PCA and connectivity matrices must be computed from training-participant data only. This correction may substantially change the unsupervised feature space and alter conclusions.

Issue 2 (participant-level leakage): Defender concedes. This is the most consequential flaw. Participant fingerprint memorization under scan-level splitting can produce near-perfect classification accuracy that entirely fails to predict unseen individuals—the actual deployment scenario. The entire split strategy must be redesigned at the participant level.
round_transcripts.defender: Both concessions stand. Issue 1 and Issue 2 together mean the experiment's performance estimates are inflated by two sources of data leakage. A participant-level split with preprocessing fitted only on training participants is necessary. Under a correctly designed experiment, the classification accuracy for both models may be substantially lower than current estimates, potentially reversing the conclusion about which approach is superior.

all_issues_adjudicated: Functional connectivity and PCA fitted on entire dataset leaks test-participant brain patterns into feature extraction; training-participant-only preprocessing is required.
Scan-level random split on a longitudinal multi-participant cohort creates participant-level leakage; all scans from a given participant must be assigned to a single partition.
